text: Digital Communication Systems  PROF. SAYED NOUH
Signal Encoding Techniques  • Digital data, Digital signals  • Digital data,Analog signals  • Analog Data, Digital Signals  • Analog Data, Analog Signals  4/18/2025  2

Signal Encoding Techniques  There are four possible combinations of data-signal encoding:   • Digital data, digital signal  • Digital data, analog signal  • Analog data, digital signal  • Analog data , analog signal  4/18/2025  3

Signal Encoding Techniques  The reason for choosing a particular combination for any given communication task vary. In the   following some representative reasons are listed:  Digital data, digital signal:   In general, the equipment for encoding digital data into digital signal is less complex and less   expensive than digital-to- analog modulation equipment.  Digital data, analog signal:   Some transmission media , such as optical fiber and wireless media, will only  propagate analog signals.  Analog data, digital signal:    Conversion of analog data to digital signal permits the use of modern digital transmission and   switching equipment, and to obtain the all advantages of the digital transmission.  Analog data, analog signal:   Analog data in electrical form can be transmitted as baseband signal easily and cheaply. This is   done with voice transmission over voice-grade line. One use common of modulation is known as   Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)  4/18/2025  4

Digital data, Digital signals  A digital signal is a sequence of discrete, discontinuous voltage pulses. Each pulse   is signal elements. Binary (digital) data are transmitted by encoding each data bit   into signal element. In the simplest case, there is a one-to-one correspondence   between bits and signal elements.  Digital signals can be categorized in three types based on the way the bits are   represented in the signals.  •Unipolar                  •Polar               • Bipolar   5  4/18/2025

Signal Encoding Techniques  Digital data, Digital signals  • Unipolar:   • As the name suggests, Unipolar signal has only one polarity. Usually positive   polarity is used for binary 1 and zero level for binary 0, Figure 2.2.a.  • Polar:   • A polar signal has two levels of opposite polarity, Figure 2.2.b.  Note that the   signals never comes back to zero voltage level. It is always in one of the two   states, +v or –v.  • Bipolar:   • A bipolar  signal  has three level  states +v , -v and zero level, Figure 2.2.c.   Bipolar signal is also termed as pseudo-ternary  because it has three level states   but use two of these to represent  the two binary digit, 0 and 1

Digital data, Digital signals  Receiving Data Bits  The signal received at the other end of the transmitting medium is never identical to   the transmitted signal as the transmission medium distorts the signal to some extent,   Figure 2.3b .There may be additional pulses due to noise spikes. As a result, the   receiver  has  to  put  in  considerable  effort  in  identifying  the  bits.  To  correctly   identifying bits, the receiver must know with some accuracy when a bit begins and   ends. That is, the receiver must know the time instant at which it should look for a   bit.  There  for,  the  receiver  must  have  synchronized  clock  pulses  which  mark  the   location of the bits  The clock should have   frequency equal to bit rate.  The received signal is sampled  using the clock pulses  7  4/18/2025

Digital data, Digital signals  What factors determine how successful the receiver will be interpreting   the incoming  signal. There are three important factors:  signal –to-   noise ratio, the data rate, and the bandwidth. With other factors held   constant, the following  statement are true:     •   An increase in data rate increases bit error rate (BER),     •   An  increase in SNR decreases bit error rate,     •   An increase in bandwidth allow an increase in data rate.  4/18/2025  8

Digital data, Digital signals  Digital signal encoding  Signal encoding refers to mapping from the data bits into digital signal. We examine the   basic task to be performed by the receiver for recovering data bits from the received   signal. The design of the receiver can be simplified by adopting robust signal encoding   scheme. The basic required characteristics of an encoded digital signal are as follow:  • The digital signal should have sufficient level transitions for the clock extraction circuit   in the receiver to work properly.  • There should not be any ambiguity in recognizing the binary states of the received   signal.  • The  interconnection signaling  links do not  provide DC  connectivity  (capacitors  are   used to block the DC component from transmitter to receiver.   • Various signal encoding schemes have different frequency spectrum characteristics, i.e.   their frequency components and amplitudes of the frequency components are different.      These characteristic should suit the transmission medium that carry the digital signal.   The signal gets distorted otherwise.  4/18/2025  9

Digital data, Digital signals  There are several signal encoding schemes which can be divided into two broad classes:  • Unipolar and polar line codes  • Bipolar line codes  o Alternate Mark Inversion (AMI)  o High Density Bipolar(HDB-3)  Unipolar and Polar Line Codes  The signal operate on two levels: line codes that use unipolar and polar signal are  of two   kinds:  • Non return to zero(NRZ) Codes  • Return to zero (RZ)Codes   4/18/2025  10

Signal Encoding Techniques  Digital data, Digital signals  Non Return to Zero (NRZ) Codes  In this class of codes, the digital signal has two different voltage levels for the two   binary digits (0,1). The signal level remains constant during a bit duration; here is no   transition (no return to zero voltage level).For example, the  absence of voltage can be   used to represent binary 0,with a constant  positive voltage used to represent   binary1.This type of coding is called unipolar.  4/18/2025  11

Signal Encoding Techniques  Digital data, Digital signals  NRZ-L: In NRZ-L (Nun return to zero voltage level) code, a bit is represented by a voltage   level which is remains constant. This code is known as unipolar.  NRZ-M and NRZ-S: In NRZ-M (Non return to zero-Mark), and     NRZ-S(Non return to   zero –space), a constant voltage pulse  is maintained for the duration of a bit time.   The NRZ codes make efficient use of bandwidth. This property is illustrated in Figure 2.5,   which compares the spectral density of various encoding schemes.  As can be seen, most of the energy in NRZ   and NRZ-I signals is between dc and half the   bit rate. For example if an NRZ code is used   to generate a signal with data rate of 9600 bps.   Most of the energy in the signal is   concentrated between dc and 4800HZ.  The main limitation of NRZ signals are   presence of a dc component and the lack of   synchronization capability.   4/18/2025  12

Signal Encoding Techniques  Digital data, Digital signals  Return To Zero (RZ) Codes  Return To Zero (RZ) codes overcome the limitation of NRZ codes in regard to   their lack of timing information. RZ codes ensure that the signal has sufficient   transition for any bit pattern. These codes are essentially a combination of   NRZ-L and the clock signal  4/18/2025  13

Signal Encoding Techniques  Digital data, Digital signals  Manchester code:   In this code, 1 is represented as logic AND of binary 1 and the clock. This produces   one clock cycle. For binary 0, the clock cycle is inverted (XNOR). Note that whatever   the bit sequence be, each bit period will have one transition in the middle. Thus the   clock extraction circuit of the receiver never faces a dearth of transition. Manchester   code is widely used in local area networks. It is also called Biphase-L code.  Biphase-M code:   In this code there is always a transition at the beginning of a bit interval. Binary 1 has   another transition in the middle of a bit interval.  Biphase-S code:   In this code also there is always a transition at the beginning of a bit interval. Binary 0   has another transition in the middle of a bit interval.  Differential Manchester code:   In this code there is always a transition in the middle of a bit interval.  4/18/2025  14

Signal Encoding Techniques  Digital data, Digital signals  The advantages of biphase schemes:  • Biphase-M, Biphase-S and Differential Manchester are differential codes and   therefore insensitive to the polarity of the received signal.  • Synchronization: Because there is predictable transition during each bit   duration, the receiver can synchronize on that transition. For this reason, the   biphase codes are known as self-clocking codes.  • Biphase codes have no dc component.  • The absence of an expected transition can be used to detect errors.  As can be seen from Figure 2.5.the bandwidth for biphase code is reasonably   narrow and contains no dc component.  4/18/2025  15

Signal Encoding Techniques  Digital data, Digital signals  Bipolar Line Codes  Bipolar signal codes are used on the metallic transition media. These codes   operate on three signal level (+v, 0, -v).  The middle level (0) represents binary 0(space). The another two levels (+v, -v)   are used for binary 1(Mark).  Bipolar codes address some of the deficiencies of the NRZ codes. Two   examples of this scheme are illustrated in Figure 2.7 Bipolar–AMI (alternate   mark inversion) and pseudo ternary.  4/18/2025  16

Signal Encoding Techniques  Digital data, Analog signal  Digital data, Analog signal  The first  attempt for establishing digital data networks were  based on using the   existing public telephone network, for digital data communication, the telephone   network was designed to receive, switch, and transmit analog  signal  in the  voice   frequency range  of about 300 to 3400HZ.It is  not at present suitable  for handling   digital signal from the subscribe  locations(although this is beginning to change),thus   digital devices are attached to the network via modem, which converts  digital data to   analog signal, and vice versa.  4/18/2025  17

Signal Encoding Techniques  Digital data, Analog signal  There are three basic types of modulation methods for transmission of digital signals.   These methods are based on the three attributes of a sinusoidal signal amplitude,   frequency, and phase. Accordingly, there are three basic encoding or modulation   techniques for transforming digital data into analog signal, as illustrated in Figure 2.8   amplitude shift keying (ASK), frequency shift keying (FSK), and phase shift keying   (PSK). In all cases, the resulting signal occupies a bandwidth centered on the carrier   frequency.  4/18/2025  18

Signal Encoding Techniques  Digital data, Analog signal  Amplitude Shift keying (ASK)  Amplitude shift keying is the simplest form of digital modulation. In ASK the   carrier amplitude is modulated by the digital input signal (binary 1 or 0), which   both the carrier frequency, fc, and phase, Ф, remain constant. The digital input   signal is a unipolar NRZ signal. Simply, we can say that in ASK the carrier   amplitude is multiplied by the binary 1 or 0 .The amplitude modulated carrier can   be written as                        S(t)=d sin(2π fc t)  4/18/2025  19

Signal Encoding Techniques  Digital data, Analog signal  The frequency spectrum of the ASK signal consists of the carrier frequency, fc, with   upper side band, (fc+R), and lower side band, (fc –R). For a random unipolar NRZ   digital signal having bit rate R, the first zero of the spectrum occurs at the R HZ away   from carrier frequency. The transmission bandwidth B of the ASK signal is restricted   by using a filter, (band pass filter), to where r is the factor related to the filter   characteristics and its value lies in the range 0-1.                          B= (1+r)R             For r=1, B =2R,            ASK is very sensitive to noise and finds limited application in data transmission.   On voice grade lines, it is typically used up to 1200bps.  4/18/2025  20

Signal Encoding Techniques  Digital data, Analog signal  Frequency shift keying (FSK)  In frequency shift keying, the two binary values (1, 0) are represented by two   different frequencies near the carrier frequency  4/18/2025  21

The FSK signal can be written as :                      S(t) = d sin(2πf1t)+d- sin (2 πf0t)  Where f1 and f0 are the frequencies corresponding to binary 1 and 0 respectively. it is   obvious that the FSK signal can be considered to be comprising of two ASK with carrier   frequencies f1 and f0. Therefore the frequency spectrum of the FSK signal can be drawn   as shown in Figure 2.12.  The estimated bandwidth is given by:          B=|f1-f0|+ (1+r)R     FSK is less susceptible to noise than ASK. On voice grade lines, it is typically used   up to 1200bps. It is also commonly used for high frequency (3 to 30 MHZ) radio   transmission. It can also be used at even higher frequencies on local area networks   that use coaxial cable.  4/18/2025  22

Signal Encoding Techniques  Digital data, Analog signal  Multiple Frequency Shift Keying (MFSK)  In Multiple frequency shift keying (MFSK) more than two frequencies are used. In this   case each signaling element represents more than one bit. The transmitted MFSK signal   for one signal element time can be defined as follow:                                     Si (t)=A sin 2πfit                   1  ≤  i  ≤ M    Where:              fi = fc + (2i-1-M) fd              fc= the un modulated carrier frequency               fd= the difference frequency              M= number of different signal elements; (i.e. number of frequency  level, 2L )              L= number of bits per signal element  4/18/2025  23

To match the data rate of the input bit stream, each output signal element is held   for a period of Ts=LT, where T is the bit period (data rate =1/T). Thus one signal   element,  which  is  a  constant  frequency  tone,  encodes  L  bits.  The  total   bandwidth  required  is  2Mfd.  It  can  be  shown  that  the  minimum  frequency   separation required is 2fd=1/Ts. Therefore, the modulator requires a bandwidth   of w = 2Mfd= M/Ts.  Example of MFSK with M=4;  With  M=4,  we  have  four  different  frequency  levels,  with  the  following   frequencies:                    f1(level 1=00)=fc-3fd                    f2(level 2=01)=fc-fd                    f3(level 3=10)=fc+fd                    f4(level 4=11)=fc+3fd`  The total bandwidth, wd=2Mfd=8fd, and each signal element with  1≤fi≤M ,   represent two bits.  4/18/2025  24

Signal Encoding Techniques  Digital data, Analog signal  Phase Shift Keying (PSK)  Phase shift keying is the most efficient of the three modulation methods and is used   for higher bit rate. In PSK, phase of the carrier is modulated to represent the binary   values. The simplest form of PSK called binary PSK (BPSK). The carrier phase is   changed between 0 and π by the polar digital signal.   4/18/2025  25

The BPSK signal can be written as                        S(t)= sin(2πfct)                                        for binary 0                        S(t)= - sin (2πfct)= sin (2πfct +π)             for binary 1   In other words,   S(t)= d sin(2πfct),                               where d= ±1.                         Expression for BPSK signal is very similar to the expression for ASK signal except   that the data   variable d  takes  the value ±1. The carrier gets suppressed due to polar   modulation signal. The frequency spectrum of the PSK signal for random NRZ digital   modulation signal is shown in Figure  The estimated bandwidth B of the BPSK signal can be obtained as before                                B=(1+r)R,                     where    0< r<1     4/18/2025  26

Multilevel  Modulation   We have so far considered the schemes for two level modulation, i.e . the bit rate and the    baud rate  are the same. Due to the limited of the telephone voice channel, the maximum   bit rate which can be achieved using any of the above two level modulation method does   not meet the requirements of the data processing community. Keeping the baud rate the   same, the bit rate can be increased using multilevel modulation. The data bits are divided   into groups of two or more bits and each group is assigned a specific state of the   sinusoidal signal.   IN the four state PSK (or  simply 4 PSK),   two bits are associated  with each phase   state. Therefore, the bit   rate is twice and   baud rate. Similarly, in 8 PSK, three bits are   associated with each phase state and,   therefore, its bit rate is three time the baud   rate.  4/18/2025  27

4PSK Modulator   The schematic of a 4 PSK modulator. It consists of two BPSK modulators. The carrier   frequency of one of the modulators is phase shifted by π/2 radians (90˚) .The data bits   are taken in the group of two bits called "dibits ", (Table 2.1), and two bipolar digital   signals are generated, one from the first bit of the dibits and the other from the second   bit the dibits and applied to the BPSK modulators. Outputs of the modulators are add to   generate 4 PSK output.   4/18/2025  28

For example, consider  A B=11  .  From Figure 2.16                          X= sin (2πfct+π),                         Y= sin (2πfct + π/2 +π)= sin (2πfct + 3/2  π)  Output:                          X  +   Y=   sin (2πfct+π) + sin (2πfct + 3/2  π)  By using the identity                                 sin  L + sin M  =  2 sin  ( L+M)  cos  (L- M)                                                                             2                   2  ∴  Output:               X + Y=  2 sin ( 2(2 πfct) + 5 π/2 )   cos  ( 2 πfct+ π - 2 πfct -3 π/2 )                                                         2                                        2               X + Y = 2 sin(2πfct+ 5π/4)  cos(-π/4)               X + Y = 2 sin(2πfct+ 5π/4)  cos(π/4)                X + Y = 2/ √ 2     sin(2πfct+ 5π/4)                X + Y =  √ 2 sin(2π fc t+ 5π/4)                X + Y = √ 2 sin(2πfct+225。)   4/18/2025  29

4 PSK Demodulator  The reference carrier (unmodulated carrier) is recovered   from the received   modulated carrier. As in modulator, a π/2 phase shifted carrier is also generated.   When these carriers are multiplied by the received signal, we get:      sin(2πfct+Ф) sin(2πfct) = 1/2 cos Ф  –  1/2 cos(4πfct+Ф)     sin(2πfct+Ф) sin(2πfct+π/2)=1/2 cos(Ф - π/2) - 1/2 cos(4πfct+Ф+π/2)  Where Ф is the phase of the received carrier  4/18/2025  30

The outputs of the multipliers are passed through low pass filters, (with cut-off-  frequency =2fc), to remove the 2fc frequency component and applied to the level   comparators which generate the dibits. Table 2.2 gives the outputs of low pass   filters for various values of input Ф.  Note that:                           U =1/2 cos(Ф - π/2)=1/2(cosФ cos π/2+sinФsinπ/2)=1/2 sinФ      And     V=1/2 cos Ф  For example:   for Ф = 3π/4 =135°             U=1/2 sin(135) = 0.35                   A=0        V=1/2 cos(135)= -0.35                   B=1                ∴    AB = 01   4/18/2025  31

Signal Encoding Techniques  Analog Data, Digital Signals  Analog Data, Digital Signals  In this section we examine the process of transforming analog data into digital   signals. Strictly speaking, it might be correct to refer to this as a process of   converting analog data into digital data: this process is known as digitalization.   Once analog data have been converted into digital data, a number of things can   happen. The three most common are as follows:  • The digital data can be transmitted using NRZ-L. In this case, we have   in fact gone directly from analog data to a digital signal.  • The digital data can be encoded as a digital signal using a code other   than NRZ-L.  • The digital data can be converted into an analog signal, using one of the   modulation techniques   4/18/2025  32

Signal Encoding Techniques  Analog Data, Digital Signals  This last seemingly curious, producer is illustrated in Figure 2.18, which shows   voice data that are digitized and then converted to an analog ASK signal. The   voice data because they have been digitized, can be treated as digital data, even   though transmission requirements (e.g., use of microwave) dictate that an analog   signal be used.  The device used for converting analog data into digital form for transmission, and   subsequently recovering the original analog data from the digital, is known as a codec   (coder-decoder). One of the most important technique used in codecs, is called “Pulse   Code Modulation”, PCM.  4/18/2025  33

Pulse Code Modulation  Pulse cod modulation (PCM) is based on the sampling theorem:  Sampling theorem: If a signal f(t) is sampled at regular intervals of time at a   rate higher than twice  the highest signal frequency, then the samples contain all   information of the original signal. The function f(t) may be reconstructed from   the samples by the  use of a low pass filter.  If voice data are limited to frequencies below 4000HZ, a conservative   procedure for intelligibility, 8000 samples per second would be sufficient to   characterize the voice signal completely. Note, however, that these are analog   samples, called pulse amplitude Modulation (PAM) samples.  To convert to digital, each of these analog samples must be assigned a binary   code.  4/18/2025  34

Figure 2.19 shows an example in which the original signal is assumed to be   bandlimited with a bandwidth B. PAM samples are taken at a rate of 2B, or   once every Ts=1/2B second. Each sample is approximated by being quantized   into one of 16 different levels. Each sample can then be represented by 4 bits.   But because the quantized values are only approximations, it is impossible to   recover the   original signal exactly. By using an 8- bit sample, which allows   256 quantizing levels. The quality of the recovered voice signal is comparable   with that achieved via analog transmission. Note that this implies that a data   rate of 8000 samples per second X 8 bits per sample = 64 kbps is needed for a   single voice signal.  4/18/2025  35

Signal Encoding Techniques  Analog Data, Digital Signals  Thus, PCM starts with a continuous time,   continuous-amplitude (analog) signal, from   which a digital signal is produced, Figure 2.20.   The digital signal consist of blocks of n bits,   where each n-bit number is the amplitude of a   PCM pulse. On reception, the process is   reversed to reproduce the analog signal. Notice,   however, that this process violates  4/18/2025  36

The terms of the sampling theorem. By quantization the PAM pulse, the original   signal is now only approximated, and cannot be recovered exactly. This effect is   known  as  quantizing  error  or  quantizing  noise.  The  signal-to-noise  ratio  for   quantizing noise can be expressed as:        SNRdB =20 log2n+1.76 dB=   20n log 2 + 1.76= 20n x 0.301 +1.76                    =6.02n+1.76  Thus each additional bit used for quantizing increases SNR by about 6 dB.  4/18/2025  37

Digital Carrier Systems  When  digital  transmission  began  emerging  as  a  feasible  technology,  CCITT                 ( Consultative Committee for International Telegraphy and Telephony ) was unable   to reach agreement on an international standard for PCM.  Consequently, there are   now  a  variety  of  incompatible  schemes  in  use  in  different  countries  around  the   world.  International hook ups between incompatible countries require “black boxes”   to convert the originating country’s system to that of the received country.  One method that is in wide spread use in North America and Japan is the T1 carrier,   depicted in fig. 3.3. (Technically speaking, the format is called DS1 and the carrier is   called  T1).    The  T1  carrier  consists  of  24  voice  channels  multiplexed  together.    Usually , the analog signals are sampled on a round – robin basis with the resulting   analog stream, PAM being fed to the codec and then merging the digital output.    Each of the 24 channels, in turn, gets to insert 8 bits into the output stream.  Seven   bits are data, and one bit is for control, yielding 7*8000 = 56,000 bps of data, and 1   * 8000 = 8000 bps of signaling information per channel.  4/18/2025  38

A  frame  consists  of  24  *  8  =  192  bits,  plus  one  extra  bit  for  frame   synchronization, yielding 193 bits every 125 µ sec.  this gives a gross data   rate of 1.544 Mbps.  The 193 rd bit is used for frame synchronization.  It takes   on the pattern 01010010…… normally, the receiver keeps checking this bit to   make sure that it has not lost synchronization.  When a T1 system is being   used entirely for data, only 23 of the channels are used for data.  The 24th one   is  used  for  special  synchronization  pattern,  to  allow  faster  recovery  in  the   event that the frame slips.  When CCITT finally did reach agreement, they felt that 8000bps of signaling   information was far too much, so its 1.544 Mbps standard is based on an 8 –   rather than a 7 – bit data item; that is the analog signal is quantized into 256   rather than 128 discrete levels.  4/18/2025  39

Two (incompatible) variations are provided:    “In common – channel signaling”: The extra bit (which is attached onto   the  rear  rather  than  the  front  of  the  193  bit  frame)  takes  on  the  value   101010….. in the odd frames and contains signaling information for all   the channels in the even frames.   “channel  associated  signaling”:  Each  channel  has  its  own  private   signaling sub channel.  A private sub channel is arranged by allocating one   of the eight user bits in every sixth frame for signaling purposes , so five   out of six samples are 8 bit wide and other one is only 7 bits wide.  4/18/2025  40

E1 Carrier System  CCITT also has a recommendation for a PCM at 2.048 Mbps called E1.   This  carrier has 32- 8-bit data samples packed into the basic 125 µ sec   frame.  Thirty of the channels are used for information and two are used for   signaling.  Each group of four frames provides 64 signaling bits, half of   which are used for channel associated signaling and the other half of which   are  used  for  frame  synchronization.    Outside  North America  and  Japan,   2.048 Mbps carrier is in wide spread use.  4/18/2025  41
